section_title: INTRODUCTION TO THE 'IF I COULD GO BACK' SERIES
content:
This is part one of a four-part series. This initial installment is designed as a commuter episode, suitable for
listening while traveling, as it is not graphically intensive, nor does it feature new charts or market maker
secret concepts being revealed. It is fundamentally a hypothetical conversation with my younger self,
addressing a frequently asked question via email: 'If I could go back in time, what would I do differently?'
This series presents that perspective, a dialogue with my younger self about what I know now.

section_title: REFLECTING ON PAST MISTAKES AND LEARNING FROM EXPERIENCE
content:
Everyone has wished they could go back in time, knowing what they know now, not just in trading but in all
facets of life. At 47, nearly 48, I have a long list of things I wish I could change, both in my trading journey
and personal life. I've shared some personal frailties on Twitter, from my younger days to where I am today.
This series aims to convey what I wish I could tell my younger self. You can use this guidance by
appreciating that I've weathered many storms, financially and personally, to gain this knowledge. Whether
you are young or older and new to markets, you will experience growing pains. While some points may be
familiar, I'll offer my personal spin on how they applied to me, serving as a history book of my struggles
during my first six years of trading development. Those first six years were plagued with every possible
mistake. This discussion will highlight major hurdles, intended as a resource to glean experience from
without enduring the suffering.

If I could sit down with my younger self, the first thing I would advise is: Don't seek approval from others.
This is a primary characteristic of a successful trader.
- Family: My family, both immediate and extended, did not share my excitement. They presented every
reason why trading was a poor, bad decision and that I would only lose money. They couldn't understand
my ambition and tried to protect me from myself. Avoid seeking their approval.
- Friends: Friends will only show interest once you start making money and they see changes in your life.
Prior to that, they may feel uncomfortable with your success because it acts as a mirror, reminding them of
opportunities they didn't pursue. Do not seek their approval.
- Co-workers: Do not tell co-workers you plan to quit your job. They will offer negative feedback, projecting
their own discontent, saying things like, 'You'll be here the rest of your life.' This will make work even more
unbearable, transforming common interests into a divide. They might even enjoy watching you fail. Keep
your plans to yourself.
- Strangers: Avoid discussing your trading aspirations with strangers. They are generally not interested
unless you're already successful, and even then, their interest is often in your earnings, not the process.
Sharing prematurely will not provide the validation you seek. Pursue this journey on your own steam and
energy. Any dependency on outside sources (family, friends, co-workers, strangers) for validation will make
the journey harder. You must make decisions independently, reap rewards, and endure losses without
external compulsion for comfort. Seek your own approval through study and development, rewarding
success and permitting yourself to be wrong for growth.

Next, learn risk management thoroughly and master it as your very first objective. You need to control your
impulses and keep your business private. Understanding the risks is paramount if you're trading with live
funds. Losing money is a 100% guarantee; no trader starts without losses.
- My first trade was a loss, 50% of my account overnight on an orange juice option. I had no idea what I
was doing and closed the account. I had to learn about charts and risk properly.
- Appreciate percentage over dollar gain or loss: Initially, I focused on specific dollar amounts, not
understanding compound interest or advanced money management like the Kelly criterion or optimal F. My
stop-loss was either impossibly wide, or I used a 'mental stop' which is unsustainable, especially when
juggling a demanding job. This led to being 'a cowboy out there riding bulls with no experience,' taking
maximum absorbable risk on every loss. It's crucial to understand that a $300 loss on a $3,000 account is a
10% loss, which is significant.
- Learn to limit trade frequency: I overtraded, feeling compelled to be in the market if it was open. If I made
a profit, I'd immediately want to re-enter, even with 20 minutes left in a session, not understanding the
increased risk at market close. More frequent trades lead to more losses, which are detrimental to a
developing trader. Limiting frequency is key.
- Avoid short-term trading turning into long-term trading: I initially had no defined trading style. When a
short-term trade moved explosively in my favor, I'd rationalize holding it for months, turning a potential short
- term gain into an eventual loss. Stick to your original objectives; scale out at your targets. Do not change
strategies mid-trade.
- Be content with 'enough': If you have a profitable week by Thursday, close shop. Don't press your luck;
you'll likely give back profits. My younger self didn't appreciate risk, believing I'd eventually be right in every
trade. I'd swing for the fences but close small profits quickly due to fatigue. This lack of appreciation for risk
will undermine your trading. You must feel the pain of a market moving against you to truly grasp risk.

Preventing life imbalances is critical, and I failed miserably here. With five children, I was consumed by the
marketplace, making money, and building businesses. I missed many of my children's sporting events,
chasing what I told myself was for them, but it was for me to escape a hated job and unsatisfactory life.
This created a 'prison cell' with the markets. I was distracted at work, worrying about commodity prices,
and equally consumed at home, neglecting my family.
- Markets are vampires: They will drain you if you let them. Despite making millions, I would give it all back
to have the time with my children and wife that I missed.
- Manage and schedule family time: Dedicate specific time for family with no markets, charts, news, or
social media. This scheduled family time is non-negotiable.
- Keep a study and trade schedule: Define specific times of day, days of the week, and months of the year
for trading and studying. Without a schedule, you risk endlessly watching charts, which can turn into
gambling. Charts can entice you to put money in, just like slot machines. Stick to your schedule.
- Off days are off days: Weekends, holidays, or low-volume periods are off days. Do not look at charts or
consume market-related content. Pour that time into your family. This prevents life imbalances.

section_title: PATIENCE IN DEVELOPMENT AND REALISTIC TIMELINES
content:
Be patient with your development. I was overzealous and unrealistic. Making money (e.g., $1,250 to
$15,000 in less than six weeks) fueled an insatiable desire for 'more.' There's no rush, especially for
younger traders (under 30), who have ample time to develop. Use this time to learn about yourself.
- Know your destructive tendencies: If you're not aware of what will derail you, no amount of learning or
capital will save you from rushing to destruction.
- Realistic timeline: My initial plan was to retire at 40, making $1,000 a month. I quickly learned this was a
low objective, but I wasn't prepared for the rapid equity growth. Making a lot of money is like a drug; it
makes you want to do more. Coupled with a lack of risk care, this led to huge gains followed by huge
drawdowns, forcing me back to the charts and away from my family.
- Personal cost: My children missed a present father; my wife missed her husband. Photographs aren't the
same as being there for milestones like a first home run or a game-winning shot. Making money on lean
hogs doesn't measure up to those missed moments.

Stop buying retail logic and tools. Retail books and courses typically teach concepts diametrically opposed
to how actual markets work. Stop wasting money.
- Books: I've bought over 2,000 books and none held the 'secret.' I wish I had stopped sooner.
- Recommended books (for money management and insight into smart money):
- Anything from Ralph Vince and Ryan Jones (optimal F, Kelly criterion
- advanced, for developed traders).
- 'How I Made $1,000,000 Trading Commodities Last Year' by Larry Williams (1970s book, still relevant for
smart money operations).
- 'Trading for a Living' by Alexander Elder.
- 'Market Wizards' (Volume 1 and 2) by Jack Schwager (for inspiration).
- 'Street Smarts' by Linda Raschke and Larry Connors.
- What not to do: John Murphy's 'Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets' is the 'retail trader's Bible.'
Learn everything in it, then do the opposite. Its high degree of failure means fading its moves often works in
your favor.

section_title: SHUN GURUS, SOCIAL MEDIA DRAINS, AND MATERIALISM
content:
Avoid looking for 'hot hand gurus.' I chased many services and hotlines early on (e.g., Larry Williams'
hotline) but consistently lost money. The problem isn't necessarily the guru, but the dependency created.
- Become your own signal generator: Do not rely on others for buy/sell signals, as it fosters codependency.
You want to be an independent thinking trader, generating your own ideas. This forces you to assume
responsibility when wrong, rather than blaming others and 'system hopping' to another 'slot machine.'
- Be willing to lose (and lose a lot) early on: It's unavoidable. No book, course, or mentor can prevent early
losses. This is why you should paper trade or demo trade. You need to experience being wrong often
without financial consequence, then correct problems before using real money. Some may take years, and
there's no shame if it's not for you. I personally suffered financially and psychologically (agoraphobia,
eating disorder) trying to avoid losses.
- Losses are normal: Losing is acceptable, part of the business. Control them, and over time, you'll make
more than you lose. My younger self saw every loss as a huge, painful event. You made a mistake, Michael,
and you'll make many more. 27 years from now, you'll look back at so many losing trades and realize they
didn't kill you. Paper trading helps you feel what it's like to be wrong a lot, and correct it.
- Stay away from social media drains: In the early days (1992), this was message boards. People's
comments influenced my decisions, both negatively ('stupid idea') and positively ('rock star'). This led me to
alter trade expectations based on others' opinions, essentially taking 'their' trade. Don't let others control
your trading.
- Don't join trading clubs: Avoid groups with a 'hive mentality.' You need to discover yourself as a trader, and
such groups stunt your growth by imposing a single approach. Trading is a solo sport. Relying on a guru or
mentor for every decision means you're not in control.
- Don't subscribe to alerts or signals: Generate your own trade ideas. You don't know the guru's motivations
or personal situation (e.g., a bad day, a spouse spat) that might influence their calls. They're human and
won't disclose these factors.
- Don't count other people's money: Avoid the popular trend of focusing on others' cars, houses, vacations,
and materialistic displays on social media (Instagram, etc.). This is a waste of time and money. If you trade
solely to achieve that lifestyle, you'll never get there, as you're focused on money, not the process. This
'dangled candy' is an illusion; 99% of these people are fakes, driving rented cars, in borrowed homes,
wearing returned clothes or fake jewelry. True success is proven by market analysis, not materialism. I
never display my wealth because it doesn't make me a better trader or student, nor does it translate into
skill. I only share what I believe is the best in market analysis concepts.

section_title: OVERCOME COMPARISON AND RECOGNIZE MARKET FRAUDS
content:
Don't feel insignificant by contrast when you see other people succeeding, or supposedly succeeding. Don't
measure yourself against them. If you do, you're implying that your success is only valid if you reach their
level. I did that until I set a goal beyond anyone I'd ever seen. I aimed to surpass Larry Williams'
achievement of taking $10,000 to $1.2 million in a year. This became my measuring stick. I didn't want to
just be 'as good as' someone else. Don't measure yourself against me, or anyone else on social media.
99% of these people are fake; they don't own the cars they drive, don't sleep in the homes they show, and
often return the clothes and wear fake watches. This is all allure to make you think they're successful. If
they were truly successful, they would prove it by showing what will happen in the marketplace, which they
strangely never do. My reputation is built on providing the best in market analysis concepts. I penned what
are now widely touted 'market-maker concepts' in 1996 (developed in 1995), proven with real money and
taught in one-on-one sessions. These concepts have spread, leading to books and courses incorporating
them, even fabricating personal narratives around my experiences. I am the only one who can prove this
stuff works. The majority of people you see out there are fakes, frauds, and not what they claim to be.

section_title: ALIGN TRADING STYLE WITH PERSONALITY AND MASTER ONE APPROACH
content:
Match your trading style to your personality. This wisdom from Larry Williams made a lot of sense, and I
wish I'd applied it sooner.
- Scalpers: Excel at quick decisions. If you hesitate, scalping or day trading will be a problem.
- Short-term traders: Typically, if you can sleep while in a trade, you're likely a good short-term trader. Don't
force yourself into a mode. Many educators try to fit everyone into a 'cookie-cutter' approach, which doesn't
work. Be original; make your own unique model.
- Don't change trading approaches (avoid system hopping): Stick to one approach, one method. Master it
and see how you work with it; this requires time. Most people don't want to put in that time. If you take a
trade and it loses, then another, don't discard the method. I did this, moving through many systems and
many trading accounts because I lacked consistency in my approach.
- Every model loses: You must accept losses; they are an unavoidable part of the business. Treat them like
a normal transaction, like missing a day of work due to sickness it's a loss, but you don't lose your job or
house. Losses are inevitable. Find your unique model, grow from it, and don't tweak it too much once it's
profitable. If you have a method that provides consistent, measurable results, combined with impeccable
risk and money management, you will become wealthy. These steps, while admittedly boring, are part of
the process.

section_title: FOCUS ON ONE TRADE AND ASSET CLASS
content:
Initially, settle on one trade setup.
- Determine your asset class: Pick one asset class (commodity, index future, Forex pair, cryptocurrency,
stock).
- Pick one market: Within that asset class, choose a single market.
- Outline buy/sell criteria: Detail the specific criteria that make you comfortable buying or selling, writing it
down as thoroughly as possible.
- Build mini study journal cases: Do not use live trades with real money. Create a study journal with a
plethora of examples where your setup worked in the past, and where it didn't. Study these extensively to
become familiar with the visual recognition of the setup. This way, you'll recognize it as live price action
forms. These steps provide a solid starting point for your trading plan. In Part 2, I will discuss insights on
studying a very particular thing: the optimal trade entry. Until then, good luck and good trading.
